web.title: Venezuelan airline moves to restart US flights - AeroTime
web.description: The move follows a February 3, 2026, filing with the US Department of Transportation in which Avior asked for approval to resume previously ...
web.markdown:
# Venezuelan airline Avior seeks return to US after five-year ban

[ByStephen Pope](https://www.aerotime.aero/articles/author/stephen-pope "View all posts by Stephen Pope")

February 10, 2026

[Airlines](https://www.aerotime.aero/category/airlines "Airlines")![avior](https://www.aerotime.aero/images/2026/02/avior-800x500.jpg)
Wikimedia Commons

Venezuelan airline Avior Airlines has asked US regulators for approval to resume flights to the United States.

The move follows a February 3, 2026, filing with the US Department of Transportation in which Avior asked for approval to resume previously approved US service and to add flights to Miami and Houston. The carrier is seeking a two-year authorization.

Some US authority already exists. Avior has long held approval for scheduled service between Barcelona, Venezuela, and Miami, as well as charter authority between the two countries.

The filing follows the Trump administration’s January 29 decision to end a five-year suspension of US-Venezuela air service.

Avior also asked regulators to act on a request it first filed in 2016 to resume service between Caracas and Miami. The department never ruled on that application. In its latest filing, the carrier is asking the Transportation Department to finally act on the request along with its current application.

Avior emphasized that it is a privately owned Venezuelan company with no financial ties to the Venezuelan government and said it remains owned and controlled by Venezuelan nationals. The carrier said it has previously been found financially fit to operate service to the United States and submitted updated financial information under seal in support of its request.

The filing comes as American Airlines recently [moved to restore service to Caracas](https://www.aerotime.aero/articles/faa-reopens-venezuelan-airspace-trump-order-american-airlines "") following the reopening of airspace.

Avior said it is ready to resume flights once approvals are granted and Venezuela’s safety rating is restored, following a US downgrade in 2019.

Avior’s fleet consists of Boeing 737-400 series aircraft, all Venezuelan-registered and owned by the airline. The carrier operates primarily within Venezuela and on some routes to Colombia.

The transportation Department has not yet responded to Avior’s application.

- [Avior Airlines](https://www.aerotime.aero/tag/avior-airlines)
- [United States](https://www.aerotime.aero/tag/united-states)
- [Venezuela](https://www.aerotime.aero/tag/venezuela)

web.title: Avior Becomes Latest Venezuelan Airline Seeking U.S. Entry
web.description: Venezuelan carrier Avior Airlines has asked the DOT to amend and renew its long-dormant authority to operate flights between Venezuela and ...
web.markdown:
https://aviationweek.com/themes/custom/particle/dist/app-drupal/assets/AW\_logo\_horizontal\_small\_white.svg

[Skip to main content](https://aviationweek.com/air-transport/airports-networks/avior-becomes-latest-venezuelan-airline-seeking-us-entry#main-content)

# Avior Becomes Latest Venezuelan Airline Seeking U.S. Entry

[David Casey](https://aviationweek.com/author/david-casey) February 04, 2026

![avior jet](https://aviationweek.com/sites/default/files/styles/crop_freeform/public/2026-02/2e4xtpm.jpg?itok=ra-adRQU)

Credit: Markus Mainka/Alamy Stock Photo

Venezuelan carrier Avior Airlines has asked the U.S. Transportation Department (DOT) to amend and renew its long-dormant authority to operate flights between Venezuela and the U.S., moving to position itself for a potential reopening of the market.

In a filing dated Feb. 3, Avior applied for an amended exemption and renewal of existing authority to operate scheduled passenger, cargo and mail services from Caracas, Maracaibo and Barcelona in Venezuela to Miami, as well as from Barcelona to Houston. The airline is seeking a two-year exemption period to allow it to resume U.S. operations once regulatory and safety requirements are met.

The application follows a Jan. 29 announcement by U.S. President Donald Trump that the U.S. would reopen commercial airspace over Venezuela and revoke a 2019 DOT order that suspended all scheduled and charter flights between the two countries on safety and security grounds. The move follows a Jan. 3 U.S. military operation that led to the capture of President Nicolás Maduro.

Avior says it is prepared to restart U.S. service “upon receipt of all necessary regulatory and safety approvals,” including Venezuela regaining FAA Category 1 safety status.

The carrier already holds exemption authority allowing scheduled service between Barcelona, Venezuela and Miami, along with broad charter rights. It has repeatedly sought to expand that authority since 2016, including a bid to add Caracas-Miami service, but those requests were never acted upon while the bilateral market remained closed.

The filing comes amid a broader flurry of activity tied to the possible normalization of U.S.–Venezuela aviation ties. In late January, American Airlines [said it was ready to resume daily service to Venezuela](https://aviationweek.com/air-transport/airports-networks/american-moves-restart-venezuela-service-after-seven-year-hiatus "https://aviationweek.com/air-transport/airports-networks/american-moves-restart-venezuela-service-after-seven-year-hiatus"), pending government approval and security assessments. American was the last U.S. carrier operating in the country before suspending flights in March 2019 and previously accounted for nearly 60% of U.S.-Venezuela seat capacity.

Venezuela’s Laser Airlines [has also revived a long-running bid to enter the U.S. market](https://aviationweek.com/air-transport/airports-networks/venezuelas-laser-airlines-revives-us-bid "https://aviationweek.com/air-transport/airports-networks/venezuelas-laser-airlines-revives-us-bid"), asking DOT to act on an application first filed in 2011. Laser has proposed scheduled service from Caracas, Valencia and Maracaibo to South Florida using MD-80 aircraft.

Before the suspension, OAG Schedules Analyser data shows more than 360,000 two-way seats were offered between the U.S. and Venezuela in 2018, while Sabre Market Intelligence recorded nearly 602,000 two-way passengers that year.

Despite the reopening of airspace, significant uncertainty remains. Any resumption of scheduled service will depend not only on DOT approvals, but also on FAA safety oversight, Transportation Security Administration requirements, and the evolving political and security situation in Venezuela.

[David Casey](https://aviationweek.com/author/david-casey)

Email: [david.casey@informa.com](mailto:david.casey@informa.com)

David Casey is Editor in Chief of Routes, the global route development community's trusted source for news and information.

- [Avior Airlines C.a.](https://aviationweek.com/term/avior-airlines-ca)
- [Venezuela](https://aviationweek.com/term/venezuela)
- [U.S. Department of Transportation](https://aviationweek.com/term/us-department-transportation)

web.markdown:

SíNo

[Publicidad](https://google.com/preferences/source?q=aviacionline.com)[Publicidad](https://www.hitit.com/)

- Avior Airlines filed a DOT application to resume scheduled passenger, cargo and mail flights between Venezuela and the U.S. following the U.S. lifting the 2019 suspension.
- Requested routes: Caracas–Miami, Maracaibo–Miami, Barcelona (Venezuela)–Houston, and renewal of Barcelona–Miami authority.
- Operations depend on regulatory and safety approvals and Venezuela obtaining FAA Category 1 status.
- Avior’s technical exhibit lists an operational fleet of eight Boeing 737-400s with specific tail numbers; additional owned aircraft exist but are not currently in service.
- Airline emphasizes private ownership and lack of Venezuelan state financial ties to satisfy U.S. fitness requirements; management led by owner Jorge Añez Dager and president Juan Bracamonte Guerrero.
- Filing cites January 29, 2026 presidential announcement and DOT Order 2026-1-24 and references the 1953 bilateral Air Transport Agreement.

2 min.

United States

**Avior Airlines** filed a formal application with the **U.S. Department of Transportation (DOT)** on Tuesday to resume scheduled passenger, cargo, and mail operations to the United States. The request comes just five days after the U.S. administration revoked the prohibition on commercial flights between the two nations.

According to docket **OST-2026-0496** accessed by our team, the Venezuelan carrier seeks to reactivate its presence in the North American market by renewing previous permits and amending its exemption authority to include new connections from Caracas and Maracaibo.

The Barcelona, Venezuela-based carrier detailed in its filing that it intends to operate the following segments:

- **¿Por qué Avior Airlines presentó la solicitud ante el DOT?**
Para reanudar operaciones regulares de pasajeros, carga y correo entre Venezuela y Estados Unidos tras el levantamiento de la suspensión de vuelos y para renovar y ampliar permisos de vuelo.

- **¿Qué rutas solicitó operar Avior?**
Caracas–Miami, Maracaibo–Miami, Barcelona (Venezuela)–Houston y la renovación de Barcelona–Miami.

- **¿Qué condiciona el inicio de las operaciones?**
La recepción de todas las aprobaciones regulatorias y de seguridad necesarias y que Venezuela reciba la calificación Category 1 por parte de la FAA.

- **¿Qué flota declaró Avior en su expediente técnico?**
Ocho Boeing 737-400 registrados con los números de cola YV3012, YV3566, YV3158, YV3011, YV3546, YV3243, YV2928 y YV3317.


- Caracas (CCS) – Miami (MIA)
- Maracaibo (MAR) – Miami (MIA)
- Barcelona (BLA) – Houston (IAH)
- Barcelona (BLA) – Miami (MIA) (renewal of existing authority)

The application is grounded in the January 29, 2026, presidential announcement and the subsequent DOT Order 2026-1-24, which lifted the suspension of air services in place since 2019. The airline argued that re-establishing these flights serves the public interest and aligns with the **1953 bilateral Air Transport Agreement**.

**Fleet and technical capacity**

In the attached technical exhibit, Avior specified an operational fleet comprising eight **Boeing 737-400** aircraft owned by the company. Registered aircraft include tail numbers YV3012, YV3566, YV3158, YV3011, YV3546, YV3243, YV2928 and YV3317. The carrier clarified it owns other aircraft not currently in service, which it will notify the DOT about if added to the schedule.

While the administrative request proceeds in Washington, actual flight operations depend on additional technical factors. The airline explicitly acknowledged in its filing that operations will commence "upon receipt of all necessary regulatory and safety approvals, and upon Venezuela receiving a Category 1 rating from the Federal Aviation Administration (FAA)".

The company’s management structure remains under the control of Venezuelan nationals, with **Jorge Añez Dager** as owner and **Juan Bracamonte Guerrero** as president. The company reiterated its private status and lack of financial ties to the Venezuelan state to meet the Department's fitness requirements.
